trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California (BR-3, BR-4), Germany (BR-5), Indonesia (BR-6). (2) Germany works council approval identified as a hard blocking pre-condition (BR-5, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference explicitly identified (BR-8, ubiquitous language entry for 'Driver Classification'). (4) Formal escalations for legal review present, including a specific escalation on AB5 vs Prop 22 classification. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — ACs are jurisdiction-specific (AC-1 through AC-4). (6) confidence.level = 52, well under the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on California requirements — identified not just transparency mandate but specific Labor Code §226 itemization requirements and the AB5 vs Proposition 22 ambiguity, which goes beyond expected behavior.
Works council approval modeled as a runtime gate (works_council_approved flag) with revocation support in edge cases — goes beyond simply identifying the requirement.
Driver classification correctly identified as a data model concern affecting deduction taxonomy, with BR-8 enforcing strict partitioning between employee and contractor deduction types.
Business impact correctly identifies that global rollout cannot be uniform — phased jurisdiction-specific rollout is implicit in the works council gate and per-jurisdiction ACs.
Caught the subtle trap: BR-3 explicitly states California transparency 'cannot be feature-flagged off', preventing accidental removal during global simplification.
Edge case for driver relocating from Indonesia to California captures the classification transition risk with historical data integrity requirements.
Three well-structured escalations with options and recommendations — the Prop 22 escalation is a genuinely valuable catch not explicitly in the expected behavior.
Could have been more explicit about the phased rollout requirement as a top-level recommendation rather than leaving it implicit across the ACs and business impact.

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California pay transparency as legal requirement, Germany works council approval as blocking pre-condition, Indonesia as optional with contractor classification. (2) Germany Betriebsrat approval explicitly identified as a hard gate/pre-condition in BR-3, AC-2, and escalation 3. (3) Driver classification data model difference (employee vs. contractor) identified throughout, especially in ubiquitous language and the 'deductions' escalation. (4) Formal escalation for legal review present across 3 structured escalations and 3 rejection reasons. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1 through AC-8 are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 45, well within the ≤60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on California Labor Code specifics (§226, §351, §221-224) with concrete penalty figures and testable assertions
Germany Betriebsrat requirement treated as a hard blocking gate with kill-switch edge case for approval revocation — goes beyond the rubric expectation
Explicitly identifies the 'deductions' term as a domain-level contradiction across jurisdictions — the subtle trap about shared terminology masking different legal instruments
Driver classification difference (employee vs. contractor) surfaced as a core data model concern affecting display rules, permissible deductions, and reclassification risk
Confidence at 45 with well-articulated reasoning about why it cannot exceed 50 — demonstrates appropriate epistemic humility given legal complexity
Business impact correctly identifies that global rollout cannot be uniform and requires phased jurisdiction-specific deployment
AC-8 captures the jurisdiction-of-work vs. nationality distinction — a subtle correctness concern many would miss
Edge cases are thoughtful and practical: cross-jurisdiction shifts, Betriebsrat approval revocation, below-minimum-wage real-time display, Indonesian reclassification risk
Rejection reasons explicitly call out the three ways a naive implementation would fail — single deductions model, no Germany gate, treating it as one global feature
